item.title: BBEdit 16 is here! | Bare Bones Software
item.description: There are more than a hundred new feature additions, changes, and refinements, including forward-looking foundational work which brings order-of-magnitude performance improvements in some parts of the product.

item.title: Hail Mary - Star Map
item.description: See a recreation of the Project Hail Mary in-ship navigation computer with accurate star data from the GAIA DR3 mission.

item.title: Why Patagonia? — Edward Champion
item.description: I feel awkward about my body, I don't feel like the picture of an outdoorsy person, and I work indoors for a living. Why do I buy Patagonia clothes? A mix of quality, warranty, and the company's commitment to its products. I like a little ethics in my clothes.

item.title: Does using LLMs make me dumber? - Wilsons Blog
item.description: So this is something I’ve heard a lot and have thought about a lot. First I want to re-frame the question from what I usually read to what I think is actually a good question: “How does using LLMs change the way I learn?” I think this is better because 1. the dumb-intelligent axis is […]

item.title: Agents Are Not One Thing
item.description: Software Engineer writing about infrastructure, Kubernetes, and Go

item.title: SpaceX's IPO Bagship carries full payload of Elon's mistakes
item.description: In which the great American space dream, it turns out, is just another vehicle where the greater fool flies coach.

item.title: Understanding KV Cache: The Hidden Memory Cost of Serving LLMs
item.description: A deep dive into KV cache — the hidden memory bottleneck of LLM inference. Learn how KV cache grows with context length and concurrency, why it matters for self-hosted models, and how attention innovations like GQA, MQA, MLA, sliding-window attention, hybrid architectures, quantization, PagedAttention, and prefix caching reduce memory pressure.

item.title: Human Bottlenecks
item.description: Why AI won't augment human capabilities much.

item.title: Highest Random Weight in Elixir | jola.dev
item.description: A description of HRW/rendezvous hashing and the HRW elixir library.
item.author: (https://jola.dev/about)

item.title: Intelligence
item.description: Some problems don’t get solved in your head, they get solved in someone else’s while you watch or read their solution. You might feel it before you understand it. A small calibration. A quiet “Oh!”

item.title: Matterhorn pandas
item.description: Matterhorn summit window from 50 years of historical weather + pandas.

item.title: 50 Hours to Draw Some Lines - Doug MacDowell
item.description: Hand drawn data visualizations by artist Doug MacDowell.

item.title: Fixing LLM writing with Distribution Fine Tuning
item.description: Technical Report: How Distribution Fine Tuning (DFT) improves LLM writing quality

item.title: The Maintainer's Dilemma | spf13
item.description: Open source maintenance is breaking at scale. Can AI code review can help maintainers without breaking trust, judgment, or community.

item.title: Containers Are a Security Boundary (some assembly required) ┈ Tianon's Ramblings ✿
item.description: I've heard "containers are not a security boundary" enough times that it's started to feel like received wisdom, and my honest read (after 13+ years) is that it's technically defensible but practically sloppy – and the sloppiness matters.

item.title: Adults Have Lost the Ability to Smile, Why?
item.description: Today at the supermarket, I was smiling at a baby. The baby saw my smile and she smiled back. I did a few more times with older kids and they all smiled back...

item.title: Lessons I Learned from Creating Searx
item.description: Seven years of maintaining a popular open source metasearch engine taught me a great deal about privacy, community, and the fundamental limits of the metasearch approach. Here is what led me to build Hister.

item.title: Back up your TOTP codes by just printing them
item.description:
Scan the Quick Response (QR) code above with your favorite Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) app. You should get a six digit number, rotating every 30 seconds, that looks something like
1 2
237601 # as of Wed May 20 09:44:53 AM EEST 2026
If you then take the secret key that QR code maps to, say

item.title: Google's Antigravity Bait and Switch
item.description: The day was to begin like any other, with Antigravity open, expecting to get some work done before my attention fragments. But Google had other plans. They had rolled out a new version of Antigravity the day before, at I/O 2026, presenting it as a shiny, standalone Codex-style experience.

item.title: no slop grenade
item.description: Stop throwing AI-generated walls of text into conversations. If they wanted an AI essay, they would have asked ChatGPT themselves.

item.title: AI is just unauthorised plagiarism at a bigger scale
item.description: AI takes in all the input, whether the original authors have consented or not, and do some "learning", and then the AI companies sell these learned result to...

item.title: Flipper One — we need your help
item.description: With Flipper One, we're reimagining what a Linux cyberdeck can be — it's a huge project. We're opening up the development process and asking the community for help.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/zhovner)

item.title: A new generation of ads for the AI era of Search
item.description: Google is introducing new ad formats built with Gemini in Search and expanding the Direct Offers pilot for shoppers.

item.title: DOS Zone | DOS games in browser
item.description: Ultimate collection of free dos games to play online in browser. No registration needed, no advertisment, just play!

item.title: GitHub - kageroumado/phosphene: A video wallpaper engine for macOS Tahoe
item.description: A video wallpaper engine for macOS Tahoe. Contribute to kageroumado/phosphene development by creating an account on GitHub.

item.title: The famous o3 "GeoGuessr" prompt did not work
item.description: In April last year, Kelsey Piper discovered that OpenAI’s o3 model was surprisingly good at figuring out where a photo was taken from. Like human “geoguessr” pros, o3 could sometimes take a nondescript photo of a beach and tell you exactly where it is. Here’s the example Kelsey gave:

item.title: Magic the Gathering format: Fun 40
182.8 Meters
